Benzo-Fused Heterocycles

The cover art illustrates an ortho-amide-functionalized diaryliodonium salt as a foundational synthetic root from which diverse benzo-fused heterocycles bloom, highlighting its significance as a versatile precursor in the synthesis of heterocycles. More in the Research Article (10.1002/adsc.70399), Yasuyuki Kita, Toshifumi Dohi, and co-workers.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Facile Synthesis of Multisubstituted Dihydroquinoxaline Derivatives via One‐Pot Sequential Ugi/Palladium‐Catalyzed Azide‐Isocyanide Coupling/Carbodiimide‐Mediated Cyclization
item.dc:description:
We developed a novel strategy for the synthesis of dihydroquinoxaline derivatives through a tandem Ugi/Pd‐catalyzed azide‐isocyanide coupling/carbodiimide‐mediated cyclization. This protocol reported the first direct synthesis of heterocycles via an intramolecular cyclization reaction between the acid protons at tertiary carbons of the Ugi adducts and carbodiimide motifs. Furthermore, the easily accessible starting materials, good yields, and high atom‐economical render this method particularly valuable in synthetic and medicinal chemistry.
A new one‐pot synthesis of multisubstituted dihydroquinoxaline derivatives via a sequential Ugi/Pd‐catalyzed azide‐isocyanide coupling/carbodiimide‐mediated cyclization process was developed. The Ugi‐4CR reactions of 2‐azidobenzenamines, acids, aldehydes, and isocyanides produced azide intermediates without separation. The reaction was followed by using another molecule of isocyanides to produce dihydroquinoxalines by the intramolecular carbodiimide‐mediated cyclization. In general, the easily accessible starting materials, one‐pot fashion, good yields, and high atom economy are the salient features of this synthetic approach.
item.content:encoded:
We developed a novel strategy for the synthesis of dihydroquinoxaline derivatives through a tandem Ugi/Pd-catalyzed azide-isocyanide coupling/carbodiimide-mediated cyclization. This protocol reported the first direct synthesis of heterocycles via an intramolecular cyclization reaction between the acid protons at tertiary carbons of the Ugi adducts and carbodiimide motifs. Furthermore, the easily accessible starting materials, good yields, and high atom-economical render this method particularly valuable in synthetic and medicinal chemistry.


A new one-pot synthesis of multisubstituted dihydroquinoxaline derivatives via a sequential Ugi/Pd-catalyzed azide-isocyanide coupling/carbodiimide-mediated cyclization process was developed. The Ugi-4CR reactions of 2-azidobenzenamines, acids, aldehydes, and isocyanides produced azide intermediates without separation. The reaction was followed by using another molecule of isocyanides to produce dihydroquinoxalines by the intramolecular carbodiimide-mediated cyclization. In general, the easily accessible starting materials, one-pot fashion, good yields, and high atom economy are the salient features of this synthetic approach.
item.dc:creator: Ying Yan, Nong‐Qi Mao, Long‐Quan Li, Ye Jin, Ping Xue, Cheng‐Ran Zhong, Jun‐Zhi Chen, Zhi‐Gang Zeng, Jun Xiong
item.dc:title: Facile Synthesis of Multisubstituted Dihydroquinoxaline Derivatives via One‐Pot Sequential Ugi/Palladium‐Catalyzed Azide‐Isocyanide Coupling/Carbodiimide‐Mediated Cyclization

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Novel Synthesis of Benzo[e][1,2,4]triazines via Cyclocondensation of Azooximes
item.dc:description:
A method for the synthesis of benzo[e][1,2,4]triazines is described. The reaction is performed under mild conditions and involves cyclocondensation of the oximino fragment onto the benzene ring.
An approach for the selective synthesis of benzo[e][1,2,4]triazines from readily accessible azooximes is described. The method is based on cyclocondensation reaction performed at room temperature in the presence of trifluoroacetic anhydride and methanesulfonic acid. The process leads to specifically substituted benzo[e][1,2,4]triazines, which can serve as precursors of stable Blatter radicals. The method features inexpensive and readily available starting materials, broad substrate scope, mild and metal‐free reaction conditions, and versatile functionalization of the 1,2,4‐benzotriazines.
item.content:encoded:
A method for the synthesis of benzo[e][1,2,4]triazines is described. The reaction is performed under mild conditions and involves cyclocondensation of the oximino fragment onto the benzene ring.


An approach for the selective synthesis of benzo[e][1,2,4]triazines from readily accessible azooximes is described. The method is based on cyclocondensation reaction performed at room temperature in the presence of trifluoroacetic anhydride and methanesulfonic acid. The process leads to specifically substituted benzo[e][1,2,4]triazines, which can serve as precursors of stable Blatter radicals. The method features inexpensive and readily available starting materials, broad substrate scope, mild and metal-free reaction conditions, and versatile functionalization of the 1,2,4-benzotriazines.
item.dc:creator: Alexander E. Frumkin, Alexander D. Dilman
item.dc:title: Novel Synthesis of Benzo[e][1,2,4]triazines via Cyclocondensation of Azooximes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Photoinduced C(sp3)–H Functionalization in Mannich Reaction
item.dc:description: Direct C(sp3)–H functionalization is an ideal topic in synthetic chemistry due to the high atom‐ and step‐economy. However, the reactivity and selectivity issues are the remaining challenges. Recently, photoinduced hydrogen atom transfer has been employed in Mannich reactions. Through direct C(sp3)‐H bond cleavage to generate alkyl radicals and subsequent reactions with imines, diverse amine scaffolds are efficiently constructed. This review summarizes recent advances in photoinduced C(sp3)–H functionalization in Mannich reactions via hydrogen atom transfer processes.
item.content:encoded: Direct C(sp3)–H functionalization is an ideal topic in synthetic chemistry due to the high atom- and step-economy. However, the reactivity and selectivity issues are the remaining challenges. Recently, photoinduced hydrogen atom transfer has been employed in Mannich reactions. Through direct C(sp3)-H bond cleavage to generate alkyl radicals and subsequent reactions with imines, diverse amine scaffolds are efficiently constructed. This review summarizes recent advances in photoinduced C(sp3)–H functionalization in Mannich reactions via hydrogen atom transfer processes.
item.dc:creator: Xiao Tang, Yongzhi Chen, Ren Kong, Shan Chang, Erik V. Van der Eycken, Liangliang Song, Liang‐An Chen
item.dc:title: Photoinduced C(sp3)–H Functionalization in Mannich Reaction

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Light‐Driven Distal Difunctionalization of Thioalkynes Using Thiosulfonates as Bifunctional Reagents
item.dc:description:
A site‐selective light‐driven 1,4−thiosulfonylation of thioalkynes with thiosulfonates as bifunctional reagents was developed.
A site‐selective light‐driven 1,4−thiosulfonylation of thioalkynes with thiosulfonates as bifunctional reagents was developed. Through a radical cascade, a panel of difunctionalized compounds has been synthesized with a complete regio‐ and diastereoselectivity for Z‐isomers (19 examples, up to 67% yield). Mechanistic studies suggested that the reaction occurred via an EnT process.
item.content:encoded:
A site-selective light-driven 1,4−thiosulfonylation of thioalkynes with thiosulfonates as bifunctional reagents was developed.


A site-selective light-driven 1,4−thiosulfonylation of thioalkynes with thiosulfonates as bifunctional reagents was developed. Through a radical cascade, a panel of difunctionalized compounds has been synthesized with a complete regio- and diastereoselectivity for Z-isomers (19 examples, up to 67% yield). Mechanistic studies suggested that the reaction occurred via an E n T process.
item.dc:creator: Balu Ramesh, Sourav Roy, Vincent Herrera, Lina Truong, Tatiana Besset
item.dc:title: Light‐Driven Distal Difunctionalization of Thioalkynes Using Thiosulfonates as Bifunctional Reagents

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Approaches for the Synthesis of Pyridine and Quinoline Cores Present in Natural Products
item.dc:description:
An anthology of the most recent methods used to synthesize pyridines and quinolines found in natural products over the last decade.
Pyridines, quinolines, and their derivatives represent a class of significant heterocyclic compounds that are prevalent in a multitude of natural products, including alkaloids found in plants, fungi, insects, marine species, and essential micronutrients such as vitamins. These aromatic systems are weak bases present in molecules with wide‐ranging biological activity, sparking the interest of scientists and pharmaceutical companies. Consequently, many drugs containing pyridines and quinolines are available on the market. Therefore, synthesizing the primary pyridine core found in various natural products is a significant challenge. Since William Ramsay first synthesized pyridine in 1876, scientists have devised numerous ingenious strategies to overcome this problem, one example being the famous Hantzsch process. The various methods that have been used over the last decade for the rapid production of these important heterocycles, which are found in natural products, are outlined in this review. These methods and strategies are crucial for synthesizing some of the complex and fascinating structures designed by Mother Nature.
item.content:encoded:
An anthology of the most recent methods used to synthesize pyridines and quinolines found in natural products over the last decade.


Pyridines, quinolines, and their derivatives represent a class of significant heterocyclic compounds that are prevalent in a multitude of natural products, including alkaloids found in plants, fungi, insects, marine species, and essential micronutrients such as vitamins. These aromatic systems are weak bases present in molecules with wide-ranging biological activity, sparking the interest of scientists and pharmaceutical companies. Consequently, many drugs containing pyridines and quinolines are available on the market. Therefore, synthesizing the primary pyridine core found in various natural products is a significant challenge. Since William Ramsay first synthesized pyridine in 1876, scientists have devised numerous ingenious strategies to overcome this problem, one example being the famous Hantzsch process. The various methods that have been used over the last decade for the rapid production of these important heterocycles, which are found in natural products, are outlined in this review. These methods and strategies are crucial for synthesizing some of the complex and fascinating structures designed by Mother Nature.
item.dc:creator: Carl Bowman, Mathieu Frenette, Sylvain Canesi
item.dc:title: Recent Approaches for the Synthesis of Pyridine and Quinoline Cores Present in Natural Products

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Stereocontrolled Glycal Functionalization by Electrochemical C2‐ Selenation
item.dc:description:
A copper‐substituted lanthanum hexaaluminate catalyst was rationally designed via controlled coprecipitation for efficient N2O abatement from ammonia oxidation reactor in nitric acid production. An optimized titration rate yields a well‐crystallized structure with abundant Cu+ sites and oxygen vacancies, enabling over 99% N2O conversion at high space velocity through a synergistic Cu redox and oxygen‐vacancy‐mediated Mars‐van Krevelen mechanism.
Sugar selenide compounds are synthetically valuable due to their ability to enable efficient, stereocontrolled glycosylation and late‐stage functionalization, while C2‐selenosugar‘s reactivity and redox behavior make them attractive scaffolds for applications in medicinal chemistry and chemical biology. Here, we report an electrochemical method for the synthesis of 2‐deoxy‐2‐selenyl glycosides from glycal through anodic oxidation of readily available aryl diselenides to generate selenium radicals, followed by selective addition. The method uses inexpensive graphite electrodes and exhibits broad functional group tolerance, accommodating diverse protecting groups as well as a wide range of alcohols and amines, including complex substrates. Mechanistic studies reveal that a C‐2 selenium substituent in sugar can coordinate to the anomeric carbocation, enabling highly selective β‐glycoside formation. The synthetic utility of this protocol is demonstrated through the efficient preparation of 2‐deoxy‐2‐selenyl thioglycosides for efficient glycosylation. Overall, this electrochemical strategy provides a valuable and sustainable platform for accessing functional 2‐deoxy‐2‐selenyl glycosides, including simple 2‐deoxy glycosides after reduction.
item.content:encoded:
A copper-substituted lanthanum hexaaluminate catalyst was rationally designed via controlled coprecipitation for efficient N2O abatement from ammonia oxidation reactor in nitric acid production. An optimized titration rate yields a well-crystallized structure with abundant Cu+ sites and oxygen vacancies, enabling over 99% N2O conversion at high space velocity through a synergistic Cu redox and oxygen-vacancy-mediated Mars-van Krevelen mechanism.


Sugar selenide compounds are synthetically valuable due to their ability to enable efficient, stereocontrolled glycosylation and late-stage functionalization, while C2-selenosugar‘s reactivity and redox behavior make them attractive scaffolds for applications in medicinal chemistry and chemical biology. Here, we report an electrochemical method for the synthesis of 2-deoxy-2-selenyl glycosides from glycal through anodic oxidation of readily available aryl diselenides to generate selenium radicals, followed by selective addition. The method uses inexpensive graphite electrodes and exhibits broad functional group tolerance, accommodating diverse protecting groups as well as a wide range of alcohols and amines, including complex substrates. Mechanistic studies reveal that a C-2 selenium substituent in sugar can coordinate to the anomeric carbocation, enabling highly selective β-glycoside formation. The synthetic utility of this protocol is demonstrated through the efficient preparation of 2-deoxy-2-selenyl thioglycosides for efficient glycosylation. Overall, this electrochemical strategy provides a valuable and sustainable platform for accessing functional 2-deoxy-2-selenyl glycosides, including simple 2-deoxy glycosides after reduction.
item.dc:creator: Debabrata Giri, Abhijit Sau
item.dc:title: Stereocontrolled Glycal Functionalization by Electrochemical C2‐ Selenation

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Mild and Metal‐Free CH Arylation of Imidazoles via In Situ Diazotization in Water
item.dc:description:
Imidazoles undergo direct C–H arylation through in situ generated diazonium salts in water, under ambient and catalyst‐free conditions. This operationally simple method avoids hazardous intermediates, tolerates sensitive functional groups, and preserves C–halogen bonds. The selective C2 arylation shows how diazonium chemistry can be used in a sustainable, practical, and broadly applicable approach.
We report a metal‐free, open‐flask protocol for the direct CH arylation of imidazoles using aryldiazonium salts generated in situ under mild aqueous conditions. The reaction proceeds efficiently at room temperature and under air, without catalysts, ligands, or photo‐/electrochemical input, and affords monoarylated imidazoles in moderate to good yields. A wide range of anilines bearing electron‐withdrawing or electron‐donating substituents, including ortho‐ and meta‐substituted derivatives, are compatible, and carbon–halogen bonds remain intact in both coupling partners. The protocol can also be extended to related azoles under slightly modified conditions. The method thus provides a practical and scalable route to functionalized heteroaryl compounds. Importantly, this environmentally benign strategy avoids the hazards of isolated diazonium salts and expands the toolbox of sustainable CH functionalization.
item.content:encoded:
Imidazoles undergo direct C–H arylation through in situ generated diazonium salts in water, under ambient and catalyst-free conditions. This operationally simple method avoids hazardous intermediates, tolerates sensitive functional groups, and preserves C–halogen bonds. The selective C2 arylation shows how diazonium chemistry can be used in a sustainable, practical, and broadly applicable approach.


We report a metal-free, open-flask protocol for the direct CH arylation of imidazoles using aryldiazonium salts generated in situ under mild aqueous conditions. The reaction proceeds efficiently at room temperature and under air, without catalysts, ligands, or photo-/electrochemical input, and affords monoarylated imidazoles in moderate to good yields. A wide range of anilines bearing electron-withdrawing or electron-donating substituents, including ortho- and meta-substituted derivatives, are compatible, and carbon–halogen bonds remain intact in both coupling partners. The protocol can also be extended to related azoles under slightly modified conditions. The method thus provides a practical and scalable route to functionalized heteroaryl compounds. Importantly, this environmentally benign strategy avoids the hazards of isolated diazonium salts and expands the toolbox of sustainable CH functionalization.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Rosadoni, Zefiro Ferraro Fano, Angela Rosa Cuzzola, Fabio Bellina
item.dc:title: Mild and Metal‐Free CH Arylation of Imidazoles via In Situ Diazotization in Water

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Iron‐Catalyzed Regioselective Double Hydrothiolation and Double Hydrophosphination Reactions Toward Acetylene C≡C Triple Bond
item.dc:description:
This work presents the first iron‐catalyzed double hydrothiolation and hydrophosphination of unsubstituted acetylene, enabling atom‐economical and regioselective heteroatom incorporation. With Cp*Fe(CO)2(Me) (Cp = η5‐C5Me5), 1,1‐selective double hydrothiolation afforded methyldithioacetals under solvent‐free conditions, while secondary phosphines gave 1,2‐bis(diarylphosphino)ethanes. Both reactions exhibit broad functional group tolerance.
The development of atom‐economical methodologies for the regioselective incorporation of sulfur and phosphorus functionalities into simple alkynes remains a longstanding challenge. Herein, we report the iron‐catalyzed double hydrothiolation and double hydrophosphination of unsubstituted acetylene, providing access to either 1,1‐addition or 1,2‐addition products with high selectivity. The use of Cp*Fe(CO)2(Me) enabled the regioselective formation of methyldithioacetals from various thiols under solvent‐free conditions, and this is the first example of 1,1‐selective double hydrothiolation of acetylene. In contrast, the reaction with secondary phosphines afforded 1,2‐bis(diarylphosphino)ethanes as sole products. These findings not only expand the synthetic utility of acetylene as a feedstock chemical but also contribute to the development of chemistry that simultaneously creates two CE bonds (E is an atom having lone pair of electrons).
item.content:encoded:
This work presents the first iron-catalyzed double hydrothiolation and hydrophosphination of unsubstituted acetylene, enabling atom-economical and regioselective heteroatom incorporation. With Cp*Fe(CO)2(Me) (Cp = η5-C5Me5), 1,1-selective double hydrothiolation afforded methyldithioacetals under solvent-free conditions, while secondary phosphines gave 1,2-bis(diarylphosphino)ethanes. Both reactions exhibit broad functional group tolerance.


The development of atom-economical methodologies for the regioselective incorporation of sulfur and phosphorus functionalities into simple alkynes remains a longstanding challenge. Herein, we report the iron-catalyzed double hydrothiolation and double hydrophosphination of unsubstituted acetylene, providing access to either 1,1-addition or 1,2-addition products with high selectivity. The use of Cp*Fe(CO)2(Me) enabled the regioselective formation of methyldithioacetals from various thiols under solvent-free conditions, and this is the first example of 1,1-selective double hydrothiolation of acetylene. In contrast, the reaction with secondary phosphines afforded 1,2-bis(diarylphosphino)ethanes as sole products. These findings not only expand the synthetic utility of acetylene as a feedstock chemical but also contribute to the development of chemistry that simultaneously creates two CE bonds (E is an atom having lone pair of electrons).
item.dc:creator: Masumi Itazaki, Tomoya Yamashita, Tomoya Nochida, Shinya Katsube, Hiroshi Nakazawa, Toshiyuki Moriuchi
item.dc:title: Iron‐Catalyzed Regioselective Double Hydrothiolation and Double Hydrophosphination Reactions Toward Acetylene C≡C Triple Bond

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Copper‐Mediated Dual Cyclization of 2‐(Alkynylaryl)Anilines with 2‐Formylbenzonitriles: Access to 3‐(1H‐Indol‐3‐Yl)Isoindolin‐1‐Ones
item.dc:description:
A convenient method is reported to assemble an array of 3‐(1H‐indol‐3‐yl)isoindolin‐1‐one derivatives via copper‐mediated dual cyclization of 2‐(alkynylaryl)anilines and 2‐formylbenzonitriles, involving an isoindolinone migration process.
Herein, we report a novel strategy for the synthesis of 3‐(1H‐indol‐3‐yl)isoindolin‐1‐ones via copper‐mediated dual cyclization of 2‐(alkynylaryl)anilines with 2‐formylbenzonitriles. Mechanistic studies reveal that the isoindolinone core is preferentially formed, and this transformation involves an unexpected isoindolinone migration process. This approach features high atom economy, broad substrate scope, and the use of inert nitrile as the nitrogen source for isoindolinone. Moreover, the practicality of this method is demonstrated by gram‐scale synthesis and late‐stage derivatizations.
item.content:encoded:
A convenient method is reported to assemble an array of 3-(1H-indol-3-yl)isoindolin-1-one derivatives via copper-mediated dual cyclization of 2-(alkynylaryl)anilines and 2-formylbenzonitriles, involving an isoindolinone migration process.


Herein, we report a novel strategy for the synthesis of 3-(1H-indol-3-yl)isoindolin-1-ones via copper-mediated dual cyclization of 2-(alkynylaryl)anilines with 2-formylbenzonitriles. Mechanistic studies reveal that the isoindolinone core is preferentially formed, and this transformation involves an unexpected isoindolinone migration process. This approach features high atom economy, broad substrate scope, and the use of inert nitrile as the nitrogen source for isoindolinone. Moreover, the practicality of this method is demonstrated by gram-scale synthesis and late-stage derivatizations.
item.dc:creator: Wenting Huang, Zan Chen, Huijiao Li, Huanfeng Jiang, Wanqing Wu
item.dc:title: Copper‐Mediated Dual Cyclization of 2‐(Alkynylaryl)Anilines with 2‐Formylbenzonitriles: Access to 3‐(1H‐Indol‐3‐Yl)Isoindolin‐1‐Ones

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: From Precursor Control to Active‐Site Synergy: Rational Design of LaCu Hexaaluminate Catalysts for Efficient N2O Decomposition
item.dc:description:
A copper‐substituted lanthanum hexaaluminate catalyst was rationally designed via controlled coprecipitation for efficient N2O abatement from ammonia oxidation reactor in nitric acid production. An optimized titration rate yields a well‐crystallized structure with abundant Cu+ sites and oxygen vacancies, enabling over 99% N2O conversion at high space velocity through a synergistic Cu redox and oxygen vacancy‐mediated Mars–van Krevelen mechanism.
Addressing the need for efficient abatement of the potent greenhouse gas N2O from nitric acid production, this work rationally designed a copper‐substituted lanthanum hexaaluminate catalyst (LaCu1) via controlled coprecipitation. The titration rate of the precipitating agent (NH4)2CO3 solution during the coprecipitation synthesis critically governs the catalyst's structure and performance. At an optimal rate of 150 mL h−1, the catalyst exhibits a well‐crystallized magnetoplumbite framework, abundant accessible Cu+ sites, and a high concentration of surface oxygen vacancies. This unique ensemble achieves over 99% N2O conversion at 650°C under a high gas hourly space velocity of 100,000 h−1. Mechanistic studies reveal that dynamic Cu+/Cu2+ redox cycling synergizes with oxygen vacancies via a Mars–van Krevelen pathway, facilitating efficient N—O bond cleavage and oxygen migration. The work establishes a clear synthesis–structure–activity correlation, providing a rational design strategy for robust, high‐temperature N2O decomposition catalysts under industrial conditions.
item.content:encoded:
A copper-substituted lanthanum hexaaluminate catalyst was rationally designed via controlled coprecipitation for efficient N2O abatement from ammonia oxidation reactor in nitric acid production. An optimized titration rate yields a well-crystallized structure with abundant Cu+ sites and oxygen vacancies, enabling over 99% N2O conversion at high space velocity through a synergistic Cu redox and oxygen vacancy-mediated Mars–van Krevelen mechanism.


Addressing the need for efficient abatement of the potent greenhouse gas N2O from nitric acid production, this work rationally designed a copper-substituted lanthanum hexaaluminate catalyst (LaCu1) via controlled coprecipitation. The titration rate of the precipitating agent (NH4)2CO3 solution during the coprecipitation synthesis critically governs the catalyst's structure and performance. At an optimal rate of 150 mL h−1, the catalyst exhibits a well-crystallized magnetoplumbite framework, abundant accessible Cu+ sites, and a high concentration of surface oxygen vacancies. This unique ensemble achieves over 99% N2O conversion at 650°C under a high gas hourly space velocity of 100,000 h−1. Mechanistic studies reveal that dynamic Cu+/Cu2+ redox cycling synergizes with oxygen vacancies via a Mars–van Krevelen pathway, facilitating efficient N—O bond cleavage and oxygen migration. The work establishes a clear synthesis–structure–activity correlation, providing a rational design strategy for robust, high-temperature N2O decomposition catalysts under industrial conditions.
item.dc:creator: Birong Wang, Lei Pan, Shiqi Wang, Yijun Tian, Qingzhan Miao, Ruinian Xu, Biaohua Chen
item.dc:title: From Precursor Control to Active‐Site Synergy: Rational Design of LaCu Hexaaluminate Catalysts for Efficient N2O Decomposition

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Benziodolium Salts: Structures and Syntheses
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes the structures, synthetic strategies, and reactivity of benziodolium salts. Recent advances enable functionalized salts (OTf, NTf2, OTs), serving as carbene precursors or in Pd‐catalyzed couplings to access polyarylated ethylenes, benzils, and conjugated dienes. Their emerging role in halogen‐bonding catalysis is also highlighted.
Vinyl aryliodonium salts, as a distinctive class of hypervalent iodine reagents, have been widely explored in their vinylations because of their unique reactivity. In recent years, much attention has been directed toward cyclic diaryliodonium salts, particularly in response to the poor atom economy of acyclic aryliodonium salts, where aryliodide motifs are discarded as byproducts in the arylations. More recently, the synthetic investigations of benziodolium salts have recently experienced a resurgence after a long period of neglect, highlighting their inherent advantages in improving atom economy. These advances have significantly broadened the substrate scope and further expanded the application potential of cyclic aryliodonium salts.
item.content:encoded:
This review summarizes the structures, synthetic strategies, and reactivity of benziodolium salts. Recent advances enable functionalized salts (OTf, NTf2, OTs), serving as carbene precursors or in Pd-catalyzed couplings to access polyarylated ethylenes, benzils, and conjugated dienes. Their emerging role in halogen-bonding catalysis is also highlighted.


Vinyl aryliodonium salts, as a distinctive class of hypervalent iodine reagents, have been widely explored in their vinylations because of their unique reactivity. In recent years, much attention has been directed toward cyclic diaryliodonium salts, particularly in response to the poor atom economy of acyclic aryliodonium salts, where aryliodide motifs are discarded as byproducts in the arylations. More recently, the synthetic investigations of benziodolium salts have recently experienced a resurgence after a long period of neglect, highlighting their inherent advantages in improving atom economy. These advances have significantly broadened the substrate scope and further expanded the application potential of cyclic aryliodonium salts.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Developments in Catalytic Metathesis Reactions Involving Alkynes: A Key Review
item.dc:description: Alkyne metathesis, the cousin of the “noble” alkene metathesis, has gained significant attention within the past two decades and has been broadly utilized in the construction of shape‐persistent molecular architectures, functional polymers, and natural products. Carbonyl‐alkyne metathesis is a type of carbon‐carbon‐forming reaction involving the construction of a CC double bond and a carbonyl group in one transformation. In contemporary synthetic chemistry, carbene intermediates have demonstrated a wide range of uses. Enyne, alkyne‐alkyne, carbyne‐alkyne, and dithiane‐alkyne metathesis reactions are effective methods for producing diverse natural and synthetic compounds with growing environmental sustainability and efficiency. Hence, the aim of this study is to describe the development of strategies for carbonyl‐alkyne, carbene‐alkyne, alkene‐alkyne, alkyne‐alkyne, carbyne‐alkyne, and dithiane‐alkyne metathesis reactions that have been reported by researchers since 2020. By examining the scope and underlying mechanism, this review offers an in‐depth look at the field's current strengths and limitations.
item.content:encoded: Alkyne metathesis, the cousin of the “noble” alkene metathesis, has gained significant attention within the past two decades and has been broadly utilized in the construction of shape-persistent molecular architectures, functional polymers, and natural products. Carbonyl-alkyne metathesis is a type of carbon-carbon-forming reaction involving the construction of a CC double bond and a carbonyl group in one transformation. In contemporary synthetic chemistry, carbene intermediates have demonstrated a wide range of uses. Enyne, alkyne-alkyne, carbyne-alkyne, and dithiane-alkyne metathesis reactions are effective methods for producing diverse natural and synthetic compounds with growing environmental sustainability and efficiency. Hence, the aim of this study is to describe the development of strategies for carbonyl-alkyne, carbene-alkyne, alkene-alkyne, alkyne-alkyne, carbyne-alkyne, and dithiane-alkyne metathesis reactions that have been reported by researchers since 2020. By examining the scope and underlying mechanism, this review offers an in-depth look at the field's current strengths and limitations.
item.dc:creator: Ravindar Lekkala, Revathi Lekkala, Yee Ling Lau
item.dc:title: Recent Developments in Catalytic Metathesis Reactions Involving Alkynes: A Key Review

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: C–S Coupling of Thiols and Aryl Chlorides via Heterogeneous Carbon Nitride Photocatalysis and Nickel
item.dc:description:
We herein present a cyanamide‐modified carbon nitride (CNCN) as both a photoredox catalyst and an adaptive solid ligand for nickel, which enables the visible‐light‐driven C‐S cross‐coupling of thiols and aryl chlorides with broad substrate scope, high efficiency and long‐term stability.
Aryl sulfides are important thioethers, but their synthesis from thiols and aryl chlorides is hindered by inert C—Cl bonds and metal‐catalysts poisoned by sulfide. Although carbon nitride is a promising all‐organic heterogeneous photocatalyst due to tunable redox potential and nitrogen‐rich surface, its application in transition‐metal catalyzed aryl chloride transformations is limited by thiyl radical polymerization and aryl radical hydrogen abstraction, both stemming from weak thiyl radical–nickel interactions. We present a cyanamide‐modified carbon nitride (CNCN)/nickel system that enables efficient C–S cross‐coupling under visible light, with CNCN acting as an effective photocatalyst and adaptive solid ligand to enhance coordination with nickel and stabilize radical intermediates. This dual‐catalyst platform achieves over 90% yield, accommodates electron‐rich aryl chlorides, demonstrates high stability and recyclability, and avoids the use of noble metals. Mechanistic studies confirm a single‐electron transfer, radical‐mediated pathway, offering a sustainable, scalable, and cost‐effective route to aryl sulfides from abundant aryl chlorides.
item.content:encoded:
We herein present a cyanamide-modified carbon nitride (CNCN) as both a photoredox catalyst and an adaptive solid ligand for nickel, which enables the visible-light-driven C-S cross-coupling of thiols and aryl chlorides with broad substrate scope, high efficiency and long-term stability.


Aryl sulfides are important thioethers, but their synthesis from thiols and aryl chlorides is hindered by inert C—Cl bonds and metal-catalysts poisoned by sulfide. Although carbon nitride is a promising all-organic heterogeneous photocatalyst due to tunable redox potential and nitrogen-rich surface, its application in transition-metal catalyzed aryl chloride transformations is limited by thiyl radical polymerization and aryl radical hydrogen abstraction, both stemming from weak thiyl radical–nickel interactions. We present a cyanamide-modified carbon nitride (CNCN)/nickel system that enables efficient C–S cross-coupling under visible light, with CNCN acting as an effective photocatalyst and adaptive solid ligand to enhance coordination with nickel and stabilize radical intermediates. This dual-catalyst platform achieves over 90% yield, accommodates electron-rich aryl chlorides, demonstrates high stability and recyclability, and avoids the use of noble metals. Mechanistic studies confirm a single-electron transfer, radical-mediated pathway, offering a sustainable, scalable, and cost-effective route to aryl sulfides from abundant aryl chlorides.
item.dc:creator: Huali Zhang, Zhu Yin, Chong Wang, Xintuo Yang, Guigang Zhang, Jiajia Cheng
item.dc:title: C–S Coupling of Thiols and Aryl Chlorides via Heterogeneous Carbon Nitride Photocatalysis and Nickel

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Iron(III) Ligand‐to‐Metal Charge Transfer Photocatalysis Platform for CH Alkylation of Heterocycles with Feedstock Carboxylic Acids
item.dc:description:
We report a photoinduced Fe–ligand‐to‐metal charge transfer (LMCT)‐enabled decarboxylative alkylation strategy employing readily available feedstock carboxylic acids under environmentally benign conditions for direct CH alkylation of heterocycles.
The direct CH alkylation of heterocycles is a valuable strategy for modifying pharmaceutically relevant molecules, yet sustainable and broadly applicable methods remain limited. We report a visible‐light‐driven Fe(III)‐ligand‐to‐metal charge transfer (LMCT) photocatalytic protocol that enables efficient decarboxylative CH alkylation of diverse heterocycles using readily available alkyl carboxylic acids as feedstock alkyl sources. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions, utilizing catalytic amounts of earth‐abundant FeCl3 and a base, with molecular oxygen serving as the terminal oxidant, to achieve C(sp2)C(sp3) bond formation. Leveraging the inner‐sphere reactivity of photoexcited Fe(III)–carboxylate complexes, the method accommodates a wide range of alkyl carboxylic acids, including acetic acid, amino acids, natural products, and drug molecules, to provide pharmaceutically relevant alkylated heterocycles. Notably, the catalytic system also enables direct methylation of structurally diverse heterocycles, offering a sustainable and practical platform for targeted molecular diversification in medicinal chemistry.
item.content:encoded:
We report a photoinduced Fe–ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT)-enabled decarboxylative alkylation strategy employing readily available feedstock carboxylic acids under environmentally benign conditions for direct CH alkylation of heterocycles.


The direct CH alkylation of heterocycles is a valuable strategy for modifying pharmaceutically relevant molecules, yet sustainable and broadly applicable methods remain limited. We report a visible-light-driven Fe(III)-ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) photocatalytic protocol that enables efficient decarboxylative CH alkylation of diverse heterocycles using readily available alkyl carboxylic acids as feedstock alkyl sources. The reaction proceeds under mild conditions, utilizing catalytic amounts of earth-abundant FeCl3 and a base, with molecular oxygen serving as the terminal oxidant, to achieve C(sp2)C(sp3) bond formation. Leveraging the inner-sphere reactivity of photoexcited Fe(III)–carboxylate complexes, the method accommodates a wide range of alkyl carboxylic acids, including acetic acid, amino acids, natural products, and drug molecules, to provide pharmaceutically relevant alkylated heterocycles. Notably, the catalytic system also enables direct methylation of structurally diverse heterocycles, offering a sustainable and practical platform for targeted molecular diversification in medicinal chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Deepti Gairola, Shubham Parihar, Sandip Murarka
item.dc:title: Iron(III) Ligand‐to‐Metal Charge Transfer Photocatalysis Platform for CH Alkylation of Heterocycles with Feedstock Carboxylic Acids

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Iodine(I)/Iodine(III) Catalysis on the Asymmetric 1,4‐Difluorination of Z/E‐Dienes: Mechanism, Selectivity Control, and Catalyst Effects
item.dc:description:
In this work, we present a combined computational and experimental study on the 1,4‐difluorination of Z/E‐dienes using an iodine(I)/iodine(III) catalytic cycle. The reaction proceeds with exclusive 1,4‐regioselectivity and selectively yields the E‐isomer. Chiral halogen bond catalysts afford the S‐enantiomer in high enantioselectivity (96% e.e., computational; 92% e.e., experimental), governed by synergistic electrostatic and hydrogen‐bonding interactions.
The iodine(I)/iodine(III) catalytic cycle, which serves as an effective approach for organic transformations, has exhibited catalytic activity comparable to that of transition metals while boasting superior sustainability and resource efficiency. In this work, we have combined quantum chemical calculations with experimental validation to establish a comprehensive mechanistic framework for iodine(I)/iodine(III)‐catalyzed diene difluorination, clarifying the regio‐, stereo‐, and enantioselectivity in this transformation. Computational studies reveal that the E‐isomer product is preferentially generated from Z/E‐dienes under ambient temperature. The reaction displays prominent regioselectivity, with 1,4‐difluorination much more favorable than the 1,2‐regioisomeric pathway. Chiral iodine(I)/iodine(III) catalysis studies show that all tested chiral halogen bond catalysts favor the formation of the S‐enantiomer (96% e.e., catl.). Further analysis indicated that the synergistic effects of electrostatic interactions and strong hydrogen bonds play a key role in promoting the preferential generation of the S‐enantiomer. Experimental observations confirm that the difluorination reaction proceeds with excellent enantioselectivity (S‐enantiomer 92% e.e., exp.) and 1,4‐regioselectivity, with the difluorinated E‐isomer product as the major product. These findings advance the fundamental understanding of halogen bond‐driven asymmetric catalysis and highlight the great potential of iodine(I)/iodine(III) catalysts as sustainable alternatives to transition metal catalysts.
item.content:encoded:
In this work, we present a combined computational and experimental study on the 1,4-difluorination of Z/E-dienes using an iodine(I)/iodine(III) catalytic cycle. The reaction proceeds with exclusive 1,4-regioselectivity and selectively yields the E-isomer. Chiral halogen bond catalysts afford the S-enantiomer in high enantioselectivity (96% e.e., computational; 92% e.e., experimental), governed by synergistic electrostatic and hydrogen-bonding interactions.


The iodine(I)/iodine(III) catalytic cycle, which serves as an effective approach for organic transformations, has exhibited catalytic activity comparable to that of transition metals while boasting superior sustainability and resource efficiency. In this work, we have combined quantum chemical calculations with experimental validation to establish a comprehensive mechanistic framework for iodine(I)/iodine(III)-catalyzed diene difluorination, clarifying the regio-, stereo-, and enantioselectivity in this transformation. Computational studies reveal that the E-isomer product is preferentially generated from Z/E-dienes under ambient temperature. The reaction displays prominent regioselectivity, with 1,4-difluorination much more favorable than the 1,2-regioisomeric pathway. Chiral iodine(I)/iodine(III) catalysis studies show that all tested chiral halogen bond catalysts favor the formation of the S-enantiomer (96% e.e., catl.). Further analysis indicated that the synergistic effects of electrostatic interactions and strong hydrogen bonds play a key role in promoting the preferential generation of the S-enantiomer. Experimental observations confirm that the difluorination reaction proceeds with excellent enantioselectivity (S-enantiomer 92% e.e., exp.) and 1,4-regioselectivity, with the difluorinated E-isomer product as the major product. These findings advance the fundamental understanding of halogen bond-driven asymmetric catalysis and highlight the great potential of iodine(I)/iodine(III) catalysts as sustainable alternatives to transition metal catalysts.
item.dc:creator: Ying Li, Didi Chen, Chang Zhao, Bo Lu, Yanli Zeng
item.dc:title: Iodine(I)/Iodine(III) Catalysis on the Asymmetric 1,4‐Difluorination of Z/E‐Dienes: Mechanism, Selectivity Control, and Catalyst Effects

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Zn(OTf)2‐Catalyzed Dehydrocoupling of Terminal Alkynes with Diisopropylaminoborane: A Modular Route to Mono‐ and Dialkynylboranes
item.dc:description:
We report a Zn(OTf)2‐catalyzed dehydrocoupling of terminal alkynes with diisopropylaminoborane (DIPOB), which enables the selective synthesis of mono‐ and dialkynylboranes under mild conditions. This method provides a versatile platform for accessing stable tetracoordinate alkynylborons while effectively suppressing hydroboration side reactions.
The controlled incorporation of alkynyl groups onto boron centers is a powerful yet challenging transformation in organoboron chemistry, particularly for the synthesis of dialkynylboranes. Here, we report a modular and efficient strategy based on Zn(OTf)2‐catalyzed dehydrocoupling of terminal alkynes with diisopropylaminoborane (DIPOB), enabling the selective formation of both mono‐ and dialkynylboranes under mild conditions. The use of DIPOB as a borylating agent offers fine‐tuned reactivity and suppresses undesired hydroboration side reactions. The resulting aminoboranes can be further converted into stable tetracoordinate boron complexes via ligand exchange to provide stable products. Mechanistic studies reveal a reversible alkyne addition step, offering insights into the dynamic behavior of these transformations. Overall, this work provides a practical and versatile platform for accessing an underexplored class of alkynylboron derivatives.
item.content:encoded:

This method provides a versatile platform for accessing stable tetracoordinate alkynylborons while effectively suppressing hydroboration side reactions.


The controlled incorporation of alkynyl groups onto boron centers is a powerful yet challenging transformation in organoboron chemistry, particularly for the synthesis of dialkynylboranes. Here, we report a modular and efficient strategy based on Zn(OTf)2-catalyzed dehydrocoupling of terminal alkynes with diisopropylaminoborane (DIPOB), enabling the selective formation of both mono- and dialkynylboranes under mild conditions. The use of DIPOB as a borylating agent offers fine-tuned reactivity and suppresses undesired hydroboration side reactions. The resulting aminoboranes can be further converted into stable tetracoordinate boron complexes via ligand exchange to provide stable products. Mechanistic studies reveal a reversible alkyne addition step, offering insights into the dynamic behavior of these transformations. Overall, this work provides a practical and versatile platform for accessing an underexplored class of alkynylboron derivatives.

item.dc:title: Zn(OTf)2‐Catalyzed Dehydrocoupling of Terminal Alkynes with Diisopropylaminoborane: A Modular Route to Mono‐ and Dialkynylboranes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Crab‐Shaped HmPy‐Sulidine: A C1‐Symmetric Bifunctional Chiral Tridentate Ligand for Asymmetric Catalysis
item.dc:description:
Herein, we developed the crab‐shape C1‐symmetric tridentate bifunctional chiral HmPy‐Sulidines ligands, which can cooperatively activate both nucleophiles and electrophiles, exhibiting superior performance in Co(II)‐catalyzed asymmetric Michael‐type Friedel‐Crafts alkylation and inverse‐electron‐demand Hetero‐Diels‐Alder reactions. Our work is the first example of the hydroxymethyl group serving as an achiral side chain for synthesis of new ligands in the asymmetric catalysis.
Herein, we developed the modular crab‐shape C1‐symmetric bifunctional chiral pyridine‐type sulidine tridentate ligands bearing a flexibile achiral hydroxymethyl group side chain (abbreviated as HmPy‐Sulidines). They are characterized by the ease of structural modification, flexibile and rigid nature, and can be synthesized in an operationally simple ono‐step reaction on a large scale from inexpensive starting materials. Furthermore, these bifunctional tridentate ligands, capable of cooperatively activating both nucleophiles and electrophiles, exhibited superior performance in Co(II)‐catalyzed asymmetric Michael‐type Friedel–Crafts alkylation and inverse‐electron‐demand Hetero‐Diels‐Alder reactions, enabling the synthesis of diverse products in high yields (up to 91%) and enantioselectivities (up to 96% ee). Especially, our work is the first example of the hydroxymethyl group as an achiral side chain for the design and synthesis of new ligands, in which a hydroxymethyl group at the 2‐position of pyridine unit participates in the coordination with Co(II) to form a five‐membered ring, and enhances reactivity as well as enantioselectivity in the asymmetric catalysis.
item.content:encoded:
Herein, we developed the crab-shape C1-symmetric tridentate bifunctional chiral HmPy-Sulidines ligands, which can cooperatively activate both nucleophiles and electrophiles, exhibiting superior performance in Co(II)-catalyzed asymmetric Michael-type Friedel-Crafts alkylation and inverse-electron-demand Hetero-Diels-Alder reactions. Our work is the first example of the hydroxymethyl group serving as an achiral side chain for synthesis of new ligands in the asymmetric catalysis.


Herein, we developed the modular crab-shape C 1-symmetric bifunctional chiral pyridine-type sulidine tridentate ligands bearing a flexibile achiral hydroxymethyl group side chain (abbreviated as HmPy-Sulidines). They are characterized by the ease of structural modification, flexibile and rigid nature, and can be synthesized in an operationally simple ono-step reaction on a large scale from inexpensive starting materials. Furthermore, these bifunctional tridentate ligands, capable of cooperatively activating both nucleophiles and electrophiles, exhibited superior performance in Co(II)-catalyzed asymmetric Michael-type Friedel–Crafts alkylation and inverse-electron-demand Hetero-Diels-Alder reactions, enabling the synthesis of diverse products in high yields (up to 91%) and enantioselectivities (up to 96% ee). Especially, our work is the first example of the hydroxymethyl group as an achiral side chain for the design and synthesis of new ligands, in which a hydroxymethyl group at the 2-position of pyridine unit participates in the coordination with Co(II) to form a five-membered ring, and enhances reactivity as well as enantioselectivity in the asymmetric catalysis.
item.dc:creator: Zhen Yao, Lin‐Lu Liu, Wen‐Wei Xia, Zi‐Hong Hu, Rui‐ Rui Li, Xiong‐Wei Liu, Wen‐Hui Zhang, Xiong‐Li Liu
item.dc:title: Crab‐Shaped HmPy‐Sulidine: A C1‐Symmetric Bifunctional Chiral Tridentate Ligand for Asymmetric Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Development of a Cu‐Catalyzed Regioselective Hydrazidation of 1,2‐Diborylalkenes
item.dc:description:
Alkene 1,2‐diboronate esters undergo a Cu‐catalyzed regioselective addition to azodicarboxylates to generate functionalized borylated hydrazides. An investigation of mono‐ versus di‐hydrazidation processes and the use of these products in synthesis are described.
We report a Cu‐catalyzed hydrazidation of 1,2‐diborylalkenes that generates densely functionalized intermediates with control of regiochemistry (29%–71%; >98:2). The products undergo a surprisingly facile second hydrazidation step, an observation that we attribute to a substrate directed transmetallation of the remaining boronate ester to Cu(II).
item.content:encoded:
Alkene 1,2-diboronate esters undergo a Cu-catalyzed regioselective addition to azodicarboxylates to generate functionalized borylated hydrazides. An investigation of mono- versus di-hydrazidation processes and the use of these products in synthesis are described.


We report a Cu-catalyzed hydrazidation of 1,2-diborylalkenes that generates densely functionalized intermediates with control of regiochemistry (29%–71%; >98:2). The products undergo a surprisingly facile second hydrazidation step, an observation that we attribute to a substrate directed transmetallation of the remaining boronate ester to Cu(II).
item.dc:creator: Kavya Gopakumar, Sam Gallagher, Guillaume Benoit, Werngard Czechtizky, Joseph P. A. Harrity
item.dc:title: Development of a Cu‐Catalyzed Regioselective Hydrazidation of 1,2‐Diborylalkenes

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Data Driven Discovery of Reductive Aminases for Arylamine Substrates
item.dc:description:
Supervised machine learning was used to identify wild‐type imine reductases for challenging arylamine reductive aminations. A K‐Nearest Neighbors model enriched highly active enzymes from untested sequence space, delivering improved catalysts for seven reactions and revealing a link between activity and evolutionary plausibility.
Reductive amination with arylamines is a valuable but challenging transformation in pharmaceutical biocatalysis. To address this gap area in the biocatalytic toolkit, supervised machine learning was used to identify untested enzymes capable of catalyzing these reactions. A dataset comprising 269 enzymes tested on 9 different reductive amination reactions was constructed and used to train a K‐Nearest‐Neighbors regression model. The model was applied to generate predictions for 6588 untested wild‐type enzymes, from which 42 top candidates were identified. It was observed that these top 42 predicted enzymes were substantially enriched in active variants compared to the training dataset, and improved variants for 7 of the 9 reactions were identified. It was also found that these top‐predicted enzymes were significantly enriched in sequences with lower ESM1b pseudoperplexity (a measure of evolutionary plausibility) despite the model not being trained on such information. The results demonstrate that supervised machine learning is an effective tool for identifying highly active wild‐type biocatalysts for difficult transformations and suggest that protein language models may also be useful in identifying such enzymes.
item.content:encoded:
Supervised machine learning was used to identify wild-type imine reductases for challenging arylamine reductive aminations. A K-Nearest Neighbors model enriched highly active enzymes from untested sequence space, delivering improved catalysts for seven reactions and revealing a link between activity and evolutionary plausibility.


Reductive amination with arylamines is a valuable but challenging transformation in pharmaceutical biocatalysis. To address this gap area in the biocatalytic toolkit, supervised machine learning was used to identify untested enzymes capable of catalyzing these reactions. A dataset comprising 269 enzymes tested on 9 different reductive amination reactions was constructed and used to train a K-Nearest-Neighbors regression model. The model was applied to generate predictions for 6588 untested wild-type enzymes, from which 42 top candidates were identified. It was observed that these top 42 predicted enzymes were substantially enriched in active variants compared to the training dataset, and improved variants for 7 of the 9 reactions were identified. It was also found that these top-predicted enzymes were significantly enriched in sequences with lower ESM1b pseudoperplexity (a measure of evolutionary plausibility) despite the model not being trained on such information. The results demonstrate that supervised machine learning is an effective tool for identifying highly active wild-type biocatalysts for difficult transformations and suggest that protein language models may also be useful in identifying such enzymes.
item.dc:creator: Caroline K. Brennan, Miguel A. Abdo, Robert A. Scism, Liu Cao, Carlos A. Martinez, Russell D. Lewis
item.dc:title: Data Driven Discovery of Reductive Aminases for Arylamine Substrates

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Divergent Synthesis of Oxindole Derivatives via Cascade Deoxygenative Friedel–Crafts Cyclization of α‐Ketoamides
item.dc:description:
A mild, metal‐free, and organocatalytic strategy is described for the divergent synthesis of C‐3 functionalized oxindoles, key structural motifs in natural products and pharmaceuticals. The transformation proceeds via a cascade deoxygenative intramolecular Friedel–Crafts alkylation enabled by Iodine and TMSOTf‐based activation modes in combination with a silane system. In situ generation of a reactive silylium ion facilitates activation of the carbonyl group, followed by regioselective cyclization, silane‐promoted deoxygenation, and nucleophilic substitution. This multifunctional catalytic system allows efficient access to a broad range of 3‐aryl‐, 3‐alkyl‐, 3‐halo‐, and 3‐hydroxy‐oxindoles in a single operation under mild conditions. The protocol exhibits broad functional group tolerance, regioselectivity, and excellent scalability and avoids the use of transition metals, superacids, and prefunctionalized substrates. Mechanistic investigations reveal distinct substrate‐dependent reaction pathways, underscoring the versatility of the silane‐mediated cascade process.
3‐Substituted oxindoles are key motifs in natural products and pharmaceuticals. Previous syntheses required prefunctionalized substrates, transition‐metal catalysts, or strong acids under harsh conditions. Herein, we report a mild, metal‐free, and efficient alternative to the Barton–McCombie reduction for the synthesis of C‐3 functionalized oxindoles via deoxygenative intramolecular Friedel–Crafts alkylation of α‐ketoamides employing Iodine and TMSOTf‐based activation modes in combination with a silane system. The process likely involves in situ silylium ion generation, regioselective cyclization, silane‐promoted deoxygenation, and rearomatization, yielding 3‐aryl/alkyl/halo/hydroxy‐oxindoles. This one‐step protocol is mild, scalable, and compatible with diverse functional groups.
item.content:encoded:
A mild, metal-free, and organocatalytic strategy is described for the divergent synthesis of C-3 functionalized oxindoles, key structural motifs in natural products and pharmaceuticals. The transformation proceeds via a cascade deoxygenative intramolecular Friedel–Crafts alkylation enabled by Iodine and TMSOTf-based activation modes in combination with a silane system. In situ generation of a reactive silylium ion facilitates activation of the carbonyl group, followed by regioselective cyclization, silane-promoted deoxygenation, and nucleophilic substitution. This multifunctional catalytic system allows efficient access to a broad range of 3-aryl-, 3-alkyl-, 3-halo-, and 3-hydroxy-oxindoles in a single operation under mild conditions. The protocol exhibits broad functional group tolerance, regioselectivity, and excellent scalability and avoids the use of transition metals, superacids, and prefunctionalized substrates. Mechanistic investigations reveal distinct substrate-dependent reaction pathways, underscoring the versatility of the silane-mediated cascade process.


3-Substituted oxindoles are key motifs in natural products and pharmaceuticals. Previous syntheses required prefunctionalized substrates, transition-metal catalysts, or strong acids under harsh conditions. Herein, we report a mild, metal-free, and efficient alternative to the Barton–McCombie reduction for the synthesis of C-3 functionalized oxindoles via deoxygenative intramolecular Friedel–Crafts alkylation of α-ketoamides employing Iodine and TMSOTf-based activation modes in combination with a silane system. The process likely involves in situ silylium ion generation, regioselective cyclization, silane-promoted deoxygenation, and rearomatization, yielding 3-aryl/alkyl/halo/hydroxy-oxindoles. This one-step protocol is mild, scalable, and compatible with diverse functional groups.

item.dc:title: Divergent Synthesis of Oxindole Derivatives via Cascade Deoxygenative Friedel–Crafts Cyclization of α‐Ketoamides

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: C‐/N‐Glycoside Synthesis Enabled by Metallaphotoredox Catalysis
item.dc:description:
Recent advances in nickel‐ and copper‐mediated metallaphotoredox catalysis for the diastereoselective synthesis of C‐ and N‐glycosides are summarized. The cooperative interplay between photoredox and transition‐metal catalytic cycles is highlighted, with emphasis on donor design, stereochemical control, and mechanistic principles.
Glycosides are essential structural motifs in natural products and therapeutic agents. Metallaphotoredox catalysis, which combines visible‐light photoredox and transition‐metal catalysis, particularly with nickel and copper, has emerged as a powerful approach for constructing glycosidic bonds under mild, redox‐neutral conditions. However, achieving stereoselective synthesis of α‐ and β‐glycosides remains a long‐standing challenge. To address this, various nickel and copper photoredox dual‐catalyzed strategies have been developed. This review highlights recent advances in nickel‐ and copper‐mediated diastereoselective C‐ and N‐glycosylations and summarizes the mechanistic principles that govern their reactivity and selectivity.
item.content:encoded:
Recent advances in nickel- and copper-mediated metallaphotoredox catalysis for the diastereoselective synthesis of C- and N-glycosides are summarized. The cooperative interplay between photoredox and transition-metal catalytic cycles is highlighted, with emphasis on donor design, stereochemical control, and mechanistic principles.


Glycosides are essential structural motifs in natural products and therapeutic agents. Metallaphotoredox catalysis, which combines visible-light photoredox and transition-metal catalysis, particularly with nickel and copper, has emerged as a powerful approach for constructing glycosidic bonds under mild, redox-neutral conditions. However, achieving stereoselective synthesis of α- and β-glycosides remains a long-standing challenge. To address this, various nickel and copper photoredox dual-catalyzed strategies have been developed. This review highlights recent advances in nickel- and copper-mediated diastereoselective C- and N-glycosylations and summarizes the mechanistic principles that govern their reactivity and selectivity.
item.dc:creator: Yanan Hu, Hai Chen, Shulei Pan, Weidong Shang
item.dc:title: C‐/N‐Glycoside Synthesis Enabled by Metallaphotoredox Catalysis

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Highly Stereoselective Synthesis of Oligosaccharides Motifs From Stichopus herrmanni Fucan Sulfate With Anticoagulant Activities
item.dc:description:
The desulfated oligosaccharide motifs 3‐mer, 5‐mer, and 10‐mer and sulfated oligosaccharide 3‐mer from Stichopus herrmanni fucan sulfate with anticoagulant activities have been efficiently synthesized through a synergistic and highly stereoselective 1,2‐cis‐fucosylation stategy.
Fucan sulfate represents an interesting target for the development of anticoagulant therapeutics. However, the heterogeneous issue of isolated fucan sulfate and structural complexity hinders structure–activity relationships studies. Herein, we report the highly stereoselective synthesis of desulfated oligosaccharide motifs 3‐mer, 5‐mer, and 10‐mer and sulfated oligosaccharide 3‐mer from Stichopus herrmanni fucan sulfate with anticoagulant activities. The synthetic approach features the following: (1) ten contiguous (1→3)‐α‐L‐Fuc linkages were efficiently assembled through the recently developed synergistic 1,2‐cis‐fucosylation method; (2) iterative 1,2‐cis‐fucosylation and selective removal of Lev group strategy was adopted to elongate the carbohydrate chain; (3) highly stereoselective installation of the strong and nucleophilic aminopentyl linker into the reducing end for further biological studies.
item.content:encoded:
The desulfated oligosaccharide motifs 3-mer, 5-mer, and 10-mer and sulfated oligosaccharide 3-mer from Stichopus herrmanni fucan sulfate with anticoagulant activities have been efficiently synthesized through a synergistic and highly stereoselective 1,2-cis-fucosylation stategy.


Fucan sulfate represents an interesting target for the development of anticoagulant therapeutics. However, the heterogeneous issue of isolated fucan sulfate and structural complexity hinders structure–activity relationships studies. Herein, we report the highly stereoselective synthesis of desulfated oligosaccharide motifs 3-mer, 5-mer, and 10-mer and sulfated oligosaccharide 3-mer from Stichopus herrmanni fucan sulfate with anticoagulant activities. The synthetic approach features the following: (1) ten contiguous (1→3)-α-L-Fuc linkages were efficiently assembled through the recently developed synergistic 1,2-cis-fucosylation method; (2) iterative 1,2-cis-fucosylation and selective removal of Lev group strategy was adopted to elongate the carbohydrate chain; (3) highly stereoselective installation of the strong and nucleophilic aminopentyl linker into the reducing end for further biological studies.
item.dc:creator: Siqi Li, Qiang Tan, Jiayi Yang, Xiufang Wang, Guozhi Xiao
item.dc:title: Highly Stereoselective Synthesis of Oligosaccharides Motifs From Stichopus herrmanni Fucan Sulfate With Anticoagulant Activities

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Boosting Photocatalytic Performance of Metal Nanoclusters by Hierarchical Structure Engineering
item.dc:description:
Hierarchical structure engineering of metal nanoclusters,comprising the metal core, metal‐ligand interface, ligand body, and cluster‐support interface,which enables atomic‐level tailoring of light absorption, charge carrier separation/migration, and catalytic active sites, offering new opportunities for boosting nanocluster‐based photocatalytic performance.
Photocatalysis is regarded as a pivotal strategy to address the global challenges of energy shortages and environmental pollution. Metal nanoclusters (MNCs), as an emerging class of nanomaterials with atomically precise structures, provide an ideal platform for understanding and optimizing photocatalytic processes at the atomic level. Although the photocatalytic performance of MNCs has become tunable via structure engineering at diverse structural hierarchies, reminiscent of those of natural proteins, there is a lack of a systematic summary of recent development in this area. In this review, we aim to bridge the structure of MNCs with their photophysical properties in varied component steps of photocatalysis, based on a hierarchical core–shell structure scheme of MNCs. This review begins with a brief discussion of the fundamental principles and component steps of photocatalytic reactions, followed by detailed analysis of photocatalytic customization strategies at different structure hierarchies of MNCs. In particular, the mechanisms and recent advances in tailoring light absorption, charge carrier separation/migration, and catalytic active sites have been summarized at the structure hierarchies of metal core, metal–ligand interface, ligand body, and cluster–support interface. We then conclude this review by our perspectives on further development of MNC‐based photocatalysts. The methodological and mechanistic advances summarized in this review should add to understanding the photocatalytic reactions at the atomic level.
item.content:encoded:
Hierarchical structure engineering of metal nanoclusters,comprising the metal core, metal-ligand interface, ligand body, and cluster-support interface,which enables atomic-level tailoring of light absorption, charge carrier separation/migration, and catalytic active sites, offering new opportunities for boosting nanocluster-based photocatalytic performance.


Photocatalysis is regarded as a pivotal strategy to address the global challenges of energy shortages and environmental pollution. Metal nanoclusters (MNCs), as an emerging class of nanomaterials with atomically precise structures, provide an ideal platform for understanding and optimizing photocatalytic processes at the atomic level. Although the photocatalytic performance of MNCs has become tunable via structure engineering at diverse structural hierarchies, reminiscent of those of natural proteins, there is a lack of a systematic summary of recent development in this area. In this review, we aim to bridge the structure of MNCs with their photophysical properties in varied component steps of photocatalysis, based on a hierarchical core–shell structure scheme of MNCs. This review begins with a brief discussion of the fundamental principles and component steps of photocatalytic reactions, followed by detailed analysis of photocatalytic customization strategies at different structure hierarchies of MNCs. In particular, the mechanisms and recent advances in tailoring light absorption, charge carrier separation/migration, and catalytic active sites have been summarized at the structure hierarchies of metal core, metal–ligand interface, ligand body, and cluster–support interface. We then conclude this review by our perspectives on further development of MNC-based photocatalysts. The methodological and mechanistic advances summarized in this review should add to understanding the photocatalytic reactions at the atomic level.
item.dc:creator: Jiayuan Sun, Yanbo Zhang, Moshuqi Zhu, Qiaofeng Yao
item.dc:title: Boosting Photocatalytic Performance of Metal Nanoclusters by Hierarchical Structure Engineering

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Exploring the Substituent and Surface Modification Effects in Pd/CeO2 Single‐Atom Catalyst for Suzuki Coupling
item.dc:description:
Surface modification of Pd/CeO2 single‐atom catalysts with benzoic acid ligands significantly enhances catalytic activity in Suzuki–Miyaura cross‐coupling. Para‐substituted benzoic acids consistently outperform the unmodified catalyst, with the highest activity observed for small substituents. The modified catalysts also improve yields across diverse aryl bromides and boronates, highlighting surface ligand engineering as a simple and effective way to increase catalytic activity.
Single‐atom catalysts (SACs) have emerged as efficient platforms for synthetic reactions such as Suzuki–Miyaura cross‐coupling. Here, we demonstrate that a coating of benzoic‐acid‐based molecules on the surface of a Pd/CeO2 SAC increases the yield of a Suzuki reaction severalfold. This effect is present for a broad variety of reactants with electron‐withdrawing and donating substituents and a range of substituted benzoic acids. We show that the electronic nature of substituents strongly affects the catalytic performance of both uncoated and coated Pd/CeO2 SACs, where moderately electron‐withdrawing groups enhance reactivity for aryl bromides, whereas electron‐donating groups are more favorable for aryl boronates. Increased activity from the benzoic acid modification is observed across a range of reactants with electron‐withdrawing and donating substituents. These findings highlight the potential for improving SAC performance using organic coatings, as well as the broad applicability of SACs in synthetic chemistry.
item.content:encoded:

Para-substituted benzoic acids consistently outperform the unmodified catalyst, with the highest activity observed for small substituents. The modified catalysts also improve yields across diverse aryl bromides and boronates, highlighting surface ligand engineering as a simple and effective way to increase catalytic activity.


Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have emerged as efficient platforms for synthetic reactions such as Suzuki–Miyaura cross-coupling. Here, we demonstrate that a coating of benzoic-acid-based molecules on the surface of a Pd/CeO2 SAC increases the yield of a Suzuki reaction severalfold. This effect is present for a broad variety of reactants with electron-withdrawing and donating substituents and a range of substituted benzoic acids. We show that the electronic nature of substituents strongly affects the catalytic performance of both uncoated and coated Pd/CeO2 SACs, where moderately electron-withdrawing groups enhance reactivity for aryl bromides, whereas electron-donating groups are more favorable for aryl boronates. Increased activity from the benzoic acid modification is observed across a range of reactants with electron-withdrawing and donating substituents. These findings highlight the potential for improving SAC performance using organic coatings, as well as the broad applicability of SACs in synthetic chemistry.
item.dc:creator: Aray Yesmakhan, Ofer Kedem
item.dc:title: Exploring the Substituent and Surface Modification Effects in Pd/CeO2 Single‐Atom Catalyst for Suzuki Coupling

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Site‐Selective Synthesis of NH‐Pyrazolo[3,4‐b]pyridines via Copper‐Catalyzed Alkenylation of NH‐3‐Aminopyrazoles with Styrene Oxides
item.dc:description:
A convenient and efficient protocol has been developed for the synthesis of pharmaceutically privileged NH‐pyrazolo[3,4‐b]pyridines from readily available aldehydes, styrene oxides, and NH‐3‐aminopyrazoles. This work discloses a site‐selective annulation strategy in which NH‐3‐aminopyrazoles serve as N′/C‐4 dinucleophiles, rather than the conventional N′/N‐2 mode, enabled by copper‐catalyzed C−H alkenylation with styrene oxides.
A convenient and efficient protocol has been developed for the synthesis of pharmaceutically privileged NH‐pyrazolo[3,4‐b]pyridines from readily available aldehydes, styrene oxides, and NH‐3‐aminopyrazoles. This work discloses a site‐selective annulation strategy in which NH‐3‐aminopyrazoles serve as N′/C‐4 dinucleophiles, rather than the conventional N′/N‐2 mode, enabled by copper‐catalyzed C−H alkenylation with styrene oxides. The operationally simple transformation exhibits broad functional group tolerance and good scalability, as further demonstrated by the late‐stage functionalization of clinically relevant drug molecules.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Thiocyanation of Unactivated Alkenes with N‐Thiocyanatosaccharin via Remote Quinoxalinone Migration under Catalyst Free Conditions
item.dc:description:
A visible‐light‐induced thiocyanation of unactivated alkenes with N‐thiocyanatosaccharin via remote quinoxalinone migration was achieved under catalyst free conditions.
A novel visible‐light‐induced thioacylation method has been developed for the functionalization of unactivated alkenes. This approach utilizes N‐thiocyanatosaccharin as a source of SCN radicals, achieved via a photoredox passway, rather than requiring a conventional radical initiator like AIBN. The reaction proceeds via remote quinoxalinone migration. This metal‐ and catalyst‐free method serves as an efficient and environmentally friendly approach for the synthesis of distal thiocyanate‐substituted ketones.
item.content:encoded:
A visible-light-induced thiocyanation of unactivated alkenes with N-thiocyanatosaccharin via remote quinoxalinone migration was achieved under catalyst free conditions.


A novel visible-light-induced thioacylation method has been developed for the functionalization of unactivated alkenes. This approach utilizes N-thiocyanatosaccharin as a source of SCN radicals, achieved via a photoredox passway, rather than requiring a conventional radical initiator like AIBN. The reaction proceeds via remote quinoxalinone migration. This metal- and catalyst-free method serves as an efficient and environmentally friendly approach for the synthesis of distal thiocyanate-substituted ketones.
