item.dc:title: Visible‐Light‐Induced Thiocyanation of Unactivated Alkenes with N‐Thiocyanatosaccharin via Remote Quinoxalinone Migration under Catalyst Free Conditions

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: A Genuine Four‐Component Spirocyclization Reaction: Synthesis of Spiro[pyrimido[1,2‐b]indazole] Ring Derivatives
item.dc:description:
This study develops a metal‐free [3+1+1+1] four‐component self‐organized spirocyclization using commercial aryl methyl ketones, 3‐aminoindazoles, aromatic aldehydes, and cyclic β‐diketones. It proceeds via parallel tandem‐generated imine/dienophile cross‐capture and aza‐Diels–Alder reaction, forming two C–C/C–N bonds and a spiro quaternary carbon with broad substrate scope and simple operation.
A [3 + 1 + 1 + 1] four‐component spirocyclization reaction has been developed for the efficient synthesis of spiro[pyrimido[1,2‐b]indazole] skeletons from commercially available aryl methyl ketones, 3‐aminoindazoles, aromatic aldehydes, and cyclic β‐diketones. This novel four‐component reaction is a self‐organizing long‐range cascade process: imine and dienophile intermediates, generated through two independent parallel tandem reaction sequences, undergo cross‐capture followed by an aza‐Diels‐Alder reaction to afford the target spirocyclic skeletons. This method features metal‐free catalysis, broad substrate compatibility, and simple operation. The reaction involves the formation of two CC bonds and two CN bonds, while constructing a congested spiro quaternary carbon center.
item.content:encoded:
This study develops a metal-free [3+1+1+1] four-component self-organized spirocyclization using commercial aryl methyl ketones, 3-aminoindazoles, aromatic aldehydes, and cyclic β-diketones. It proceeds via parallel tandem-generated imine/dienophile cross-capture and aza-Diels–Alder reaction, forming two C–C/C–N bonds and a spiro quaternary carbon with broad substrate scope and simple operation.


A [3 + 1 + 1 + 1] four-component spirocyclization reaction has been developed for the efficient synthesis of spiro[pyrimido[1,2-b]indazole] skeletons from commercially available aryl methyl ketones, 3-aminoindazoles, aromatic aldehydes, and cyclic β-diketones. This novel four-component reaction is a self-organizing long-range cascade process: imine and dienophile intermediates, generated through two independent parallel tandem reaction sequences, undergo cross-capture followed by an aza-Diels-Alder reaction to afford the target spirocyclic skeletons. This method features metal-free catalysis, broad substrate compatibility, and simple operation. The reaction involves the formation of two CC bonds and two CN bonds, while constructing a congested spiro quaternary carbon center.
item.dc:creator: Rong‐He Wu, Yong‐Dong Du, Yong‐Xing Tang, Zhong‐Qin Gou, Wen Ye, Shi‐Peng Zou, Wen‐Han Xu, Jun‐Gang Wang, An‐Xin Wu
item.dc:title: A Genuine Four‐Component Spirocyclization Reaction: Synthesis of Spiro[pyrimido[1,2‐b]indazole] Ring Derivatives

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Detangle the Competing Sequence of 1e and 2e Oxidative Addition in Nickel(I)‐Catalyzed Radical Coupling Reactions
item.dc:description:
The reactivity difference of Ni(I) bromide and alkyl Ni(I) in 2e oxidative addition was systematically investigated through density functional theory (DFT) calculation. Computational studies revealed that alkyl Ni(I) has a higher reactivity because the alkyl ligand confers the Ni(I) center with an enhanced charge transfer degree.
Nickel‐catalyzed radical coupling reactions with electrophiles provide an intriguing strategy for radical functionalization. This type of reaction consists of two competing oxidative addition steps, i.e., the one‐electron oxidative addition of radical to Ni(I) and the two‐electron oxidative addition of electrophile to Ni(I). The competition outcome unambiguously determines both the optimal reaction mechanism and the enantiocontrol model. Theoretical studies were performed to detangle the competing sequence of one‐electron and two‐electron oxidative addition steps. Computational results revealed that the one‐electron oxidative addition of radical to Ni(I) bromide, followed by 1e reduction to generate alkyl Ni(I) intermediate, occurs preferentially before the two‐electron oxidative addition of electrophile to Ni(I) bromide. The higher reactivity of the alkyl Ni(I) complex compared to Ni(I) bromide in the rate‐determining two‐electron oxidative addition guaranteed the superiority of this NiI/NiII/NiI/NiIII catalytic cycle. The quantitative steric‐electronic effect dissection (QSED) analysis has pinpointed that the more significant charge transfer stabilization is the dominant factor conferring higher reactivity to the alkyl Ni(I) complex. Natural population analysis (NPA) demonstrates that the stronger electron‐donating ability of the alkyl ligand compared to the bromide ligand increases the electron density at the nickel center of alkyl Ni(I), thereby facilitating the charge transfer to the electrophile.
item.content:encoded:

Computational studies revealed that alkyl Ni(I) has a higher reactivity because the alkyl ligand confers the Ni(I) center with an enhanced charge transfer degree.


Nickel-catalyzed radical coupling reactions with electrophiles provide an intriguing strategy for radical functionalization. This type of reaction consists of two competing oxidative addition steps, i.e., the one-electron oxidative addition of radical to Ni(I) and the two-electron oxidative addition of electrophile to Ni(I). The competition outcome unambiguously determines both the optimal reaction mechanism and the enantiocontrol model. Theoretical studies were performed to detangle the competing sequence of one-electron and two-electron oxidative addition steps. Computational results revealed that the one-electron oxidative addition of radical to Ni(I) bromide, followed by 1e reduction to generate alkyl Ni(I) intermediate, occurs preferentially before the two-electron oxidative addition of electrophile to Ni(I) bromide. The higher reactivity of the alkyl Ni(I) complex compared to Ni(I) bromide in the rate-determining two-electron oxidative addition guaranteed the superiority of this NiI/NiII/NiI/NiIII catalytic cycle. The quantitative steric-electronic effect dissection (QSED) analysis has pinpointed that the more significant charge transfer stabilization is the dominant factor conferring higher reactivity to the alkyl Ni(I) complex. Natural population analysis (NPA) demonstrates that the stronger electron-donating ability of the alkyl ligand compared to the bromide ligand increases the electron density at the nickel center of alkyl Ni(I), thereby facilitating the charge transfer to the electrophile.
item.dc:creator: Minghao Xu, Jing Zhang, Xiao Shen, Juan Wang, Xiaotian Qi
item.dc:title: Detangle the Competing Sequence of 1e and 2e Oxidative Addition in Nickel(I)‐Catalyzed Radical Coupling Reactions

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Cascade Copper‐Catalyzed C–P Coupling/Knoevenagel Cyclization/Photocyclization for Diversity‐Oriented Synthesis of Luminescent Phosphole Derivatives
item.dc:description:
Benzo‐ and dibenzophosphole oxides are valuable for organic electronics, but their site‐specific functionalization and the construction of diverse fused frameworks remain challenging. This paper reports a tandem copper‐catalyzed C–P coupling/Knoevenagel cyclization/photocyclization with excellent chemoselectivity. This Knoevenagel‐type cyclization is unprecedented in benzophosphole oxide synthesis, providing a new disconnection that enables programable, site‐specific functionalization of benzo[b]phosphole oxides and their efficient assembly from readily accessible starting materials. Systematic photophysical investigations further reveal substituent‐governed modulation of optical performance.
Benzo‐ and dibenzophosphole oxides have attracted increasing attention in organic electronics. However, their functionalization with site‐specific substituents and the construction of diverse fused phospholes remain formidable challenges. Herein, we present a cascade of copper‐catalyzed C–P coupling/Knoevenagel cyclization/photocyclization for the precise and programable construction of site‐specifically substituted benzo[b]phosphole, heteroarene‐fused phosphole, and polycyclic phosphole oxides. This reliable and efficient method boasts excellent substrate scope, straightforward operation, and readily available starting materials, enabling the concise synthesis of diverse (hetero)aryl‐fused phosphole derivatives, as well as the precise functionalization of both the benzo moiety and the 2,3‐positions. Furthermore, systematic investigation into their photophysical properties has elucidated the substituent‐governed modulation of optical performance.
item.content:encoded:
Benzo- and dibenzophosphole oxides are valuable for organic electronics, but their site-specific functionalization and the construction of diverse fused frameworks remain challenging. This paper reports a tandem copper-catalyzed C–P coupling/Knoevenagel cyclization/photocyclization with excellent chemoselectivity. This Knoevenagel-type cyclization is unprecedented in benzophosphole oxide synthesis, providing a new disconnection that enables programable, site-specific functionalization of benzo[b]phosphole oxides and their efficient assembly from readily accessible starting materials. Systematic photophysical investigations further reveal substituent-governed modulation of optical performance.


Benzo- and dibenzophosphole oxides have attracted increasing attention in organic electronics. However, their functionalization with site-specific substituents and the construction of diverse fused phospholes remain formidable challenges. Herein, we present a cascade of copper-catalyzed C–P coupling/Knoevenagel cyclization/photocyclization for the precise and programable construction of site-specifically substituted benzo[b]phosphole, heteroarene-fused phosphole, and polycyclic phosphole oxides. This reliable and efficient method boasts excellent substrate scope, straightforward operation, and readily available starting materials, enabling the concise synthesis of diverse (hetero)aryl-fused phosphole derivatives, as well as the precise functionalization of both the benzo moiety and the 2,3-positions. Furthermore, systematic investigation into their photophysical properties has elucidated the substituent-governed modulation of optical performance.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Fundamentals, Emerging Regulation Strategies of Porphyrin‐Structured Materials for Enhanced Photocatalysis
item.dc:description:
This review summarizes the recent structural regulation strategies of porphyrin‐based structural materials for enhanced photocatalytic performance, including central metal selection, ligand modification, and π–π supramolecular self‐assembly. Moreover, the structure‐activity relationship between porphyrin structure and photocatalytic performance is reviewed, providing a rational guidance for the design of high‐efficiency porphyrin‐based photocatalysts.
Porphyrin‐based structural materials and their derivatives play a crucial role in the field of photocatalysis, thanks to their unique conjugated macroring structure, tunable electronic properties, and biomimetic catalytic capabilities. By precisely modifying the structure of porphyrins, it is possible to significantly enhance their light absorption, charge separation, and catalytic performance, thereby advancing their applications in photocatalysis. This review systematically explores the structural control methods for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of porphyrin‐structured materials and their photocatalytic mechanisms, including the selection mechanism of the central metal, the ligand modification strategies, and the self‐assembly techniques. It also elaborates on the impact of these strategies on key performance aspects such as photocatalytic CO2 reduction, water splitting, pollutant degradation, and organic synthesis. Furthermore, it presents prospects for the possible future research directions of porphyrin‐structured materials in photocatalytic applications.
item.content:encoded:

Moreover, the structure-activity relationship between porphyrin structure and photocatalytic performance is reviewed, providing a rational guidance for the design of high-efficiency porphyrin-based photocatalysts.

Furthermore, it presents prospects for the possible future research directions of porphyrin-structured materials in photocatalytic applications.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Organic Synthesis Enabled by Rotating Magnetic Field and Metal Rods: Magnetoredox and Mechanochemical Milling Reactions
item.dc:description: The development of sustainable synthetic methodologies remains a central goal in modern chemistry. Recent advances in photocatalysis, mechanochemistry, and electrochemistry have each provided unique advantages. Magnetic fields offer distinctive opportunities for organic synthesis as a complementary approach. This review summarizes recent advances in organic transformations enabled by rotating magnetic fields and metal rods from two perspectives: (1) magnetoredox reactions via electromagnetic induction, and (2) mechanochemical milling reactions.

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: A New Synthetic Approach to the Preparation of Indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines From Sulfonylalkyl Indoles
item.dc:description:
Functionalized indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines can be efficiently prepared from sulfonylalkyl indoles by a cascade process involving reduction, vinylogous aza‐Michael and aromatization reactions triggered by zinc powder in acetic acid at reflux.
The reductive cyclization of sulfonylalkyl indoles obtained from 2‐(o‐nitroaryl)indoles, provides an efficient preparation of functionalized indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines. This cascade process promoted by zinc powder in acetic acid at reflux, involves a sequential nitro group reduction, a vinylogous aza‐Michael reaction, and a final aromatization.
item.content:encoded:
Functionalized indolo[3,2-c]quinolines can be efficiently prepared from sulfonylalkyl indoles by a cascade process involving reduction, vinylogous aza-Michael and aromatization reactions triggered by zinc powder in acetic acid at reflux.


The reductive cyclization of sulfonylalkyl indoles obtained from 2-(o-nitroaryl)indoles, provides an efficient preparation of functionalized indolo[3,2-c]quinolines. This cascade process promoted by zinc powder in acetic acid at reflux, involves a sequential nitro group reduction, a vinylogous aza-Michael reaction, and a final aromatization.

item.dc:title: A New Synthetic Approach to the Preparation of Indolo[3,2‐c]quinolines From Sulfonylalkyl Indoles

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Nickel‐Catalyzed Ortho‐C(sp3/sp2) Functionalization of Free Halobenzoic Acids
item.dc:description: We report a Ni(0)‐catalyzed radical‐mediated ortho‐alkylation of 2‐halobenzoic acids that effectively addresses the challenges of over‐alkylation and β‐hydride elimination. Katritzky's salts derived from readily available primary amines are an excellent source of alkyl radicals. A diverse array of aryl and styryl incorporation was realized by employing Ni(0) and Pd(0)‐catalyzed Suzuki coupling. Control experiments provided mechanistic insights and highlighted the chelation‐promoted ortho‐functionalization of benzoic acids.
item.content:encoded: We report a Ni(0)-catalyzed radical-mediated ortho-alkylation of 2-halobenzoic acids that effectively addresses the challenges of over-alkylation and β-hydride elimination. Katritzky's salts derived from readily available primary amines are an excellent source of alkyl radicals. A diverse array of aryl and styryl incorporation was realized by employing Ni(0) and Pd(0)-catalyzed Suzuki coupling. Control experiments provided mechanistic insights and highlighted the chelation-promoted ortho-functionalization of benzoic acids.

item.dc:title: Nickel‐Catalyzed Ortho‐C(sp3/sp2) Functionalization of Free Halobenzoic Acids

item.source: Wiley: Advanced Synthesis & Catalysis: Table of Contents
item.title: Recent Advances in Atomically Precise Silver Clusters Applied for Electrocatalysis: From Synthesis and Structure to Catalysis
item.dc:description:
Atomically precise silver clusters provide a molecular‐level platform for electrocatalyst design. This graphical abstract highlights how chemical reduction, ligand engineering, in situ activation, support engineering, and self‐assembly regulate the structure, active sites, and interfacial microenvironment of silver clusters, thereby enabling their applications in oxygen reduction, CO2 reduction, hydrogen evolution, and electrocatalytic organic hydrogenation.
Atomically precise silver nanoclusters with discrete and crystallographically resolved structures are emerging as a versatile platform to bridge nanoscience and electrocatalysis. Their designable metal cluster cores, ligand shells, and higher order assemblies enable systematic regulation of electronic structure, active‐site accessibility, and interfacial microenvironments, offering opportunities to mitigate intrinsic limitations of bulk Ag (e.g., weak intermediate adsorption and competition of side reactions). Yet, the controllable synthesis of well‐defined Ag clusters remains challenging, and potential‐driven operando evolution also complicates the identification of true working active motifs. In this review, we summarize recent advances in the synthesis and structural modulation of atomically precise Ag clusters, and highlight representative electrocatalytic applications in oxygen reduction reaction, CO2RR, hydrogen evolution reaction, and electrocatalytic hydrogenation. Particular emphasis is placed on ligand‐shell engineering (lability, steric effect, and surface coverage), support coupling/defect effects, and operando activation or reconstruction that collectively govern the activity, selectivity, and durability of catalysts. We hope this overview provides practical guidance and design inspiration to develop silver cluster‐based electrocatalysts and to establish clear relationships between structure/interface and performance in this rapidly developing field.
item.content:encoded:
Atomically precise silver clusters provide a molecular-level platform for electrocatalyst design. This graphical abstract highlights how chemical reduction, ligand engineering, in situ activation, support engineering, and self-assembly regulate the structure, active sites, and interfacial microenvironment of silver clusters, thereby enabling their applications in oxygen reduction, CO2 reduction, hydrogen evolution, and electrocatalytic organic hydrogenation.


Atomically precise silver nanoclusters with discrete and crystallographically resolved structures are emerging as a versatile platform to bridge nanoscience and electrocatalysis. Their designable metal cluster cores, ligand shells, and higher order assemblies enable systematic regulation of electronic structure, active-site accessibility, and interfacial microenvironments, offering opportunities to mitigate intrinsic limitations of bulk Ag (e.g., weak intermediate adsorption and competition of side reactions). Yet, the controllable synthesis of well-defined Ag clusters remains challenging, and potential-driven operando evolution also complicates the identification of true working active motifs. In this review, we summarize recent advances in the synthesis and structural modulation of atomically precise Ag clusters, and highlight representative electrocatalytic applications in oxygen reduction reaction, CO2RR, hydrogen evolution reaction, and electrocatalytic hydrogenation. Particular emphasis is placed on ligand-shell engineering (lability, steric effect, and surface coverage), support coupling/defect effects, and operando activation or reconstruction that collectively govern the activity, selectivity, and durability of catalysts. We hope this overview provides practical guidance and design inspiration to develop silver cluster-based electrocatalysts and to establish clear relationships between structure/interface and performance in this rapidly developing field.

item.dc:title: Recent Advances in Atomically Precise Silver Clusters Applied for Electrocatalysis: From Synthesis and Structure to Catalysis
